subject: people who married their high school sweetheart
tagline: Fitbit for people who married their high school sweetheart
one_liner: Continuous biometric monitoring for couples with a sample size of one.
brand_voice_notes: Faux-clinical wellness corporate. The premise (marrying at seventeen means you now share one circulatory system) is never acknowledged as strange. Numbers are precise, recommendations are absent, and the tone is that of a device company that read one paper on attachment theory and immediately built hardware. Copy never winks. The band never judges. It just vibrates.

hero.h1: You chose each other at seventeen. Verify it daily.
hero.sub: Steady is the first wearable designed for people who married their high school sweetheart. Two bands, one merged heart rate, and a streak counter that goes back to homecoming.

features.title: One Baseline
features.blurb: Separate heart rates imply separate people. Steady merges your cardiac data into a single Couple Pulse, sampled every four seconds. Individual readings remain available on request, in writing, co-signed.

features.title: Retroactive Streaks
features.blurb: Upload one yearbook photo and our dating team backfills your activity streak to your first slow dance. The current record is 11,942 consecutive days. Those users receive nothing. But they know.

features.title: Roads Not Taken™
features.blurb: The band vibrates twice when you pass within 500 feet of anyone you almost dated between 1996 and 2008. No action is recommended. It just vibrates.

pricing_tiers.name: Going Steady
pricing_tiers.tagline: For couples who still think in shared-locker terms.
perks: Streak insurance: one skipped evening walk per fiscal year

pricing_tiers.name: The Long Haul
pricing_tiers.tagline: You survived one semester of long distance. This is nothing.
perks: Reunion Mode: suppresses all metrics for one weekend per decade

pricing_tiers.name: Till Death
perks: Streak transfer to next of kin

testimonials.quote: Our Couple Pulse dropped nine points during his fantasy draft. We discussed it calmly, and now we don't discuss it.
testimonials.name: Dana Wheeless
testimonials.title: Married 21 years to her lab partner, Class of '05

testimonials.quote: The band vibrated twice outside a Costco and I never found out who it was. I think about it a normal amount.
testimonials.name: Marcus Tefft
testimonials.title: Regional claims adjuster, married the girl from homeroom 114

testimonials.quote: We backdated our streak to the 1997 winter formal. Our children treat us differently now. Better.
testimonials.name: Lorrie Anne Baxter
testimonials.title: Co-chair, Class of '99 reunion committee, 27 years running

demo_screen_concept: A shared-vitals dashboard for one paired couple: a large merged Couple Pulse number with a live ECG trace, a streak counter reading 6,847 days since Homecoming 2007 with a 'protect streak' action, a Roads Not Taken alert feed listing timestamped near-encounters with almost-dated classmates (each dismissible via a 'noted' button), a joint sleep-debt ledger, and a locked Individual Mode toggle that opens an upsell modal requiring both spouses' written consent.

images.rationale: hero skipped: editorial-typographic direction (ceremonial announcement hero, ECG flourish drawn in CSS) needs no photo; demo assets skipped: demo_screen_concept is a vitals dashboard (data plates, alert feed), not photo-shaped; full budget on 3 deadpan testimonial headshots for the guestbook entries

items.prompt: Deadpan corporate headshot, editorial photograph: an American woman in her early forties with a composed, faintly proud expression, shoulder-length brown hair, sensible dusty-rose blouse, photographed head-and-shoulders against a warm ivory studio backdrop with a very soft muted rose gradient (warm ivory paper tone, hint of dusty rose, deep claret burgundy styling accent), soft even key light, single focal subject, calm suburban-professional energy. No text, no letters, no numbers, no logos, no watermarks, no rendered UI screens.

Deadpan corporate headshot, editorial photograph: an American man in his mid forties with a neutral, mildly preoccupied expression, short neat hair, grey suit jacket over a plain shirt, regional insurance-office energy, photographed head-and-shoulders against a warm ivory studio backdrop with a very soft muted rose gradient (warm ivory paper tone, hint of dusty rose, deep claret burgundy styling accent), soft even key light, single focal subject. No text, no letters, no numbers, no logos, no watermarks, no rendered UI screens.

Deadpan corporate headshot, editorial photograph: an American woman in her late forties with an efficient, quietly triumphant expression, styled blonde bob, pearl earrings, structured burgundy cardigan, longtime volunteer-committee-chair energy, photographed head-and-shoulders against a warm ivory studio backdrop with a very soft muted rose gradient (warm ivory paper tone, hint of dusty rose, deep claret burgundy styling accent), soft even key light, single focal subject. No text, no letters, no numbers, no logos, no watermarks, no rendered UI screens.

design_direction.style: Editorial anniversary-announcement medical brochure, pressed-flower daylight mode. The page is staged as the launch material of a heavily funded couples-wearable company that formats everything like a wedding announcement and specifies everything like a Class II medical device. A warm ivory paper ground carries thin ornamental hairline frames, small-caps eyebrows, and a single recurring ECG flourish: a hand-steady cardiogram line that stands in wherever another brand would put a divider, a heart, or a flourish of ivy. Panels are flat announcement cards with 1px burgundy hairlines, 4px radii, and no shadows deeper than a stationery emboss. One deep claret burgundy carries the wordmark, headlines, hairline frames, and all ceremonial ink; one rationed dusty rose is reserved strictly for the single primary CTA per section, the live Couple Pulse trace, and the Roads Not Taken alert affordance, and appears almost nowhere else. Cormorant Garamond sets the wordmark and headlines in high-contrast romantic serif with the cadence of a newspaper wedding announcement that keeps citing sensor accuracy; Mulish carries plain, faintly regulatory body copy. Every figure that pretends to be biometric telemetry (merged BPM, streak days, proximity distances, tier prices, consent counts) is set in IBM Plex Mono with tabular figures inside small hairline chips so it reads like device output, not sentiment. Motion is ceremonial and slight: the hero ECG line draws itself once on load, announcement cards rise 6px and fade in on scroll, the streak counter settles upward once, the CTA presses down 1px, and the Couple Pulse breathes on a 4s cycle in the demo, all gated behind prefers-reduced-motion. No dark mode, no glass, no clay, no neon, no bento lattice, no condensed signage caps, no marquee. Reads like a device company that believes a marriage that started in homeroom is, clinically speaking, one patient.
design_direction.archetype: Couples-wearable landing staged as an anniversary announcement crossed with a device brochure: centered ceremonial hero framed in hairlines with an ECG flourish and a merged-vitals plate, features as numbered announcement clauses, testimonials as guestbook entries with headshots, pricing as anniversary tiers, a single rose CTA band, and a demo handoff to the shared-vitals dashboard.
design_direction.motion_vocab: Hero ECG line draws once on load; announcement cards rise 6px and fade in on scroll; the streak counter settles upward once; the CTA presses down 1px; the Couple Pulse breathes on a 4s cycle in the demo; everything gated behind prefers-reduced-motion.
design_direction.hero_composition_notes: Centered ceremonial announcement inside a thin double hairline frame, like an invitation that passed FDA review: small-caps eyebrow (EST. HOMECOMING), giant Cormorant headline, one drawn ECG flourish separating headline from subcopy, rose CTA pair, and below the frame a flat vitals plate mock showing the merged Couple Pulse in mono with a rose trace.
